ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for scallop vermicelli as a composite dish. FOODON_03315595 (scallop food product) used as closest parent. Vermicelli component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers scallop as a raw ingredient. No composite dish code exists for scallop vermicelli. Used as closest parent anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for scallop vermicelli composite dish. No suitable parent concept identified without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of one jumbo Canadian scallop (approximately 60–80 g) placed on its shell atop a nest of soaked mung bean vermicelli (approximately 40–50 g), dressed with garlic-soy sauce and steamed, yielding a total edible portion of roughly 150 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Scallops, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175177) and Noodles, chinese, cellophane or long rice (mung beans), dry (FDC ID 169726); values combined by weighted proportion (55% scallop cooked, 30% vermicelli cooked, 15% sauce/condiments) and adjusted for cooking yield.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for jumbo Canadian scallop vermicelli as a composite dish. Values estimated by weighted combination: ~55% steamed scallop (USDA FDC 175177, cooked yield applied), ~30% cooked mung bean vermicelli (USDA FDC 169726, rehydrated), ~15% garlic-soy-sesame sauce contribution. Canadian scallops (Placopecten magellanicus) are nutritionally comparable to mixed-species scallop data in USDA SR Legacy. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce seasoning. Confidence: medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Mung bean noodles / cellophane noodles (GI ~26–33, mean ~28); scallop component contributes negligible glycemic load.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for jumbo Canadian scallop vermicelli as a composite dish. GI estimated from the dominant glycemic component: mung bean glass noodles (cellophane noodles), which have a published GI of approximately 26–33 (mean ~28) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Scallop and sauce components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. GI of 26 used (lower bound of published range, conservative estimate). GL per 150 g serving = (26 × 17.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 4.5.

hypertension.guidance: Each 150 g serving provides approximately 630 mg sodium, representing about 27% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by health authorities; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or consume this dish occasionally and balance with low-sodium foods throughout the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index mung bean vermicelli with high-protein scallop
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~26) of mung bean glass noodles combined with the high protein content of scallop (~10 g per 100 g) supports a moderate postprandial glucose response, making this dish a suitable occasional choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled to approximately 150 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, lean seafood protein, moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Scallops are a lean seafood with low saturated fat (~0.3 g per 100 g) and provide omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids; however, the soy sauce seasoning contributes moderate sodium (~420 mg per 100 g), so individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake and request lighter seasoning where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jumbo Canadian Scallop Vermicelli is a Thai-style steamed seafood dish featuring a large Atlantic sea scallop (Placopecten magellanicus) sourced from Canadian waters, placed on its half-shell atop a nest of soaked mung bean glass noodles (vermicelli), and steamed with a fragrant garlic-soy-sesame sauce. The dish is finished with finely sliced spring onion and sometimes a touch of chilli oil. The scallop is prized for its sweet, delicate flavour and tender, slightly firm texture, while the glass noodles absorb the savoury umami-rich sauce and become silky and translucent. This preparation is popular in Thai seafood restaurants in Singapore, where premium imported scallops are a signature offering. Nutritionally, the dish is a high-protein, low-fat, moderate-carbohydrate meal with a low glycemic index driven by the mung bean noodle base. Each serving provides approximately 15 g of protein, 17 g of carbohydrate, and under 3 g of fat. The sodium content is moderate to high due to soy sauce seasoning. Clinically, the dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high protein content, and is a lean seafood option for cardiovascular health, though sodium should be monitored for hypertensive individuals. The scallop contributes vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and zinc.
common_user_queries: What is jumbo Canadian scallop vermicelli?
How many calories in scallop glass noodle?
Is scallop vermicelli good for diabetics?
What are the nutrients in steamed scallop with glass noodles?
Is Canadian scallop vermicelli high in sodium?
What type of noodle is used in scallop vermicelli?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for jumbo Canadian scallop vermicelli as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central entries for raw scallop (FDC 175177) and mung bean cellophane noodles (FDC 169726), adjusted for cooking yield and sauce contribution. GI is estimated from the mung bean glass noodle component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Canadian scallops are nutritionally comparable to USDA mixed-species scallop data. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (scallop FDC 175177 + mung bean noodle FDC 169726) using a weighted proportion model (~55% cooked scallop, ~30% cooked vermicelli, ~15% sauce). No direct approved-source entry for this composite dish exists. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this estimation. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for both scallop and mung bean noodle components. 4. GI of 26 is an analogous estimate based on mung bean glass noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists. 5. Sodium value (~420 mg per 100 g) reflects soy sauce seasoning contribution and is an estimate; actual sodium will vary by restaurant preparation. 6. The dish is served at Shrimp Prawn Seafood - Boat Quay, Singapore, as a Thai-style seafood offering. Price SGD 37.80 per serving. 7. Halal status is assumed based on ingredients (scallop, mung bean noodles, soy sauce, garlic, sesame); no formal halal certification confirmed for the specific restaurant.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full composite nutrient profile from USDA FoodData Central weighted estimation. Added Thai native-script local name and aliases. Added Chinese simplified and traditional names. Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg). Confirmed trans_fat_g 0.0 is confirmed zero per USDA source. Set GI to 26 from mung bean glass noodle analogous estimate. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. Confirmed all ontology match_type values use only allowed strings.

Iteration 2: Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases to address the Singaporean context for multilingual naming compliance (G6, Q1, Q8). Ensured consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases list. Verified all hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria.

Iteration 3: Fixed q8 failure — corrected Tamil alias in aliases_by_language from mixed-script string containing a Devanagari character (ஸकाலप्) to pure Tamil script (ஸ்காலப்), ensuring exact character-for-character match with the corresponding entry in the flat aliases list. All aliases_by_language entries now verified verbatim against flat aliases array.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
